title: Quasilinear evolution versus von Neumann selective measurement
abstract: In this article, we introduce a new form of quantum selective measurement in which the von Neumann projection postulate is replaced by quasilinear evolution, governed by a nonlinear generalization of the von Neumann equation. We demonstrate that this equation preserves the equivalence of quantum ensembles and, consequently, satisfies the no-signalling principle, ensuring consistency with both quantum mechanics and Einstein causality. Our approach eliminates the need for instantaneous, discontinuous state collapse and provides a unified description of the postmeasurement quantum state reduction as a form of quantum state evolution. Notably, it does not require invoking concepts such as the quantum state assigned to a classical apparatus. At the same time, the stochastic character of selective measurement and the Born rule remain unchanged. We present several numerical solutions of the evolution equation for quasilinear selective measurement in two-level quantum systems and compare them with the standard von Neumann projection. The results demonstrate agreement between the two measurement schemes in their fundamental properties. Furthermore, we investigate phenomena associated with the structural instability of the evolution equation and identify very narrow parameter regions in which the outcomes deviate from those predicted by the von Neumann projection. These regions may offer opportunities to test the proposed approach experimentally. Finally, using specific analytical solutions, we discuss the Stern-Gerlach experiment within the framework of quasilinear measurement.
summary: This paper replaces the instantaneous von Neumann collapse with a continuous quasilinear evolution that describes state reduction as a smooth dynamical process. The proposed model preserves fundamental quantum principles like the no-signaling principle and the Born rule while identifying specific parameter regimes where the model might deviate from standard theory, offering potential experimental tests.
main_problem: Addressing the conceptual issues of the von Neumann projection postulate, specifically the instantaneous and discontinuous nature of state collapse.
main_result: The authors propose a quasilinear evolution that provides a continuous, deterministic description of state reduction that is consistent with the no-signaling principle and the Born rule.
method: The study uses a nonlinear generalization of the von Neumann equation, employing Bloch-vector representation, SL(2,C) group invariants, and numerical simulations of Riccati-type equations.
model_or_system: A two-level quantum system (qubit) undergoing evolution driven by a time-dependent potential, with specific applications to the Stern-Gerlach experiment.
key_observables: Spin projection (observable Omega), Bloch vector trajectories, and the rate of change of the state vector.
important_parameters: The driving potential magnitude (g0), the orientation angle (Theta), and the Casimir invariants (C1, C2) of the evolution.
main_assumptions: The validity of the model relies on the preservation of ensemble equivalence to ensure consistency with Einstein causality.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the parameter space as a tetrahedron, the evolution of Bloch vectors under different potentials (Morse and Stern-Gerlach), and the rate of state change in various regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the quasilinear framework, proves its consistency with no-signaling and the Born rule, analyzes the dynamics of two-level systems using geometric invariants, explores structural instability and experimental testability, and applies the model to the Stern-Gerlach experiment.
why_interesting: It offers a novel way to unify unitary evolution with measurement-induced reduction through a continuous nonlinear dynamical process, which is highly relevant to the foundations of open quantum systems and measurement theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The two columns show the dynamics conditioned on two possible measurement outcomes, λ = ±1. In each plot, the horizontal axis indicates the time t on a logarithmic scale. The red, green, and blue lines denote the components of the state vector n(t); the thin straight lines in the same colors denote the respective components of the vector πλ = λˆω representing the state resulting from the von Neumann projection.
